g.data-narration: Welcome to Boolean Logical Operators in Java. This module covers all six operators needed for decision-making in code.
Boolean logical operators work with true/false values and form the foundation of decision-making in every Java program.
The boolean primitive type holds only true or false. All logical operators work exclusively with boolean values or expressions.
The NOT operator (!) is a unary operator that inverts boolean value. True becomes false, false becomes true.
The AND operator returns true only when BOTH operands are true. Even one false makes the entire expression false.
g.text: 2. Logical AND Operator (&) - Both Must Be True

g.data-narration: The OR operator returns true if AT LEAST ONE operand is true. Only false when BOTH are false.
g.text: 3. Logical OR Operator (|) - At Least One True

g.data-narration: XOR returns true ONLY when EXACTLY ONE operand is true. It returns false when both are the same (both true or both false).
Short-circuit AND (&&) works like & but OPTIMIZES by stopping if first operand is false. This saves computation time.
g.text: If first operand is false, second is

g.data-narration: Short-circuit OR (||) stops evaluating if first operand is true. Since result is determined, second operand is skipped.
g.text: If first operand is true, second is

g.data-narration: The main difference is short-circuit evaluation. Use && and || for better performance and to prevent errors like NullPointerException.
g.text: && and || stop early when result is determined

g.data-narration: This example demonstrates short-circuit behavior. With &&, the second method is skipped when first returns false.
g.text: Output: With && only "First method called" prints. With &, both methods are called.

g.data-narration: Short-circuit operators prevent NullPointerException. When checking null before accessing methods, && ensures safe evaluation order.
XOR is useful for toggle operations. Pressing XOR true flips the state, and pressing again returns to original value.
Operator precedence determines evaluation order. NOT has highest precedence, followed by AND, then XOR, then OR.
Here is the complete summary of all six boolean logical operators with their symbols and behavior.
For exams, memorize truth tables and understand short-circuit behavior. These are the most frequently tested concepts.
Avoid common mistakes like confusing & with && and forgetting that XOR returns false when both operands are true.
We covered six boolean operators: NOT, AND, OR, XOR, and their short-circuit variants. Use && and || for better performance.
